questions.question: Why is data protection central to modern application security?

choices.text: Modern applications often depend on stored information such as passwords, financial records, and messages.
Modern applications avoid databases by keeping all information only in temporary memory.
Data protection matters only when applications are disconnected from the internet.
Stored data is safe by default once it is written to a server disk.
Application security is mostly unrelated to how information is stored.

questions.explanation: Applications are valuable because of the data they store and process. Protecting that data is therefore not a separate concern from application security; it is one of its core goals.

questions.question: After data is stored, what must still be ensured?

choices.text: Only that the database uses a familiar brand name.
Availability, accuracy over time, and protection from unauthorized access.
Only that the data can be exported to a spreadsheet.
Only that cloud infrastructure replaces local infrastructure.
Only that passwords and messages are stored in the same table.

questions.explanation: Storing data is just the starting point. A secure data system must keep information available, correct, and confidential against mistakes and attacks.

questions.question: Why do database management and cloud infrastructure need to be considered together?

choices.text: Cloud infrastructure removes all database security responsibilities from the organization.
Databases become unnecessary once applications move to the cloud.
Database structure and protection still matter, but cloud hosting changes the security responsibilities and risks.
Cloud providers protect only physical disks and never expose APIs.
Cloud security is unrelated to internal mistakes or external attacks.

questions.explanation: Modern data security spans both the database itself and the infrastructure where it runs. Moving to cloud changes control boundaries, but it does not remove the need for database protection.

questions.question: Which set best captures the protection scope for modern data systems?

choices.text: Only hard drive replacement and office network cabling.
Only cloud billing and provider marketing claims.
Only user interface design and application color themes.
Table structure, internal permissions, external attacks, encryption, and cloud responsibilities.
Only backup copies, with no access control or attack prevention.

questions.explanation: Data security is layered. Reliable storage, structured access, attack prevention, encryption, and cloud governance each protect against different failure modes.

questions.question: Which failure best shows why availability, accuracy, and authorization must all be protected together?

choices.text: A database table has a primary key and a foreign key.
A cloud provider offers both public and private cloud options.
A DBMS provides a standardized way to retrieve information.
A developer uses a view to hide sensitive columns.
A database stays online, but a race condition sells the same airline seat twice and unauthorized users can view the records.

questions.explanation: A system can fail in multiple dimensions at once. Being online is not enough if data becomes inaccurate or visible to the wrong people, which is why database security combines availability, integrity, and confidentiality concerns.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: Why do important applications need a dedicated data system instead of a simple text file?

choices.text: A dedicated system organizes, finds, and protects data efficiently.
A text file automatically prevents all hard drive failures.
A text file provides role-based permissions by default.
A dedicated system removes the need for backups and access control.
A text file can safely handle every concurrent transaction.

questions.explanation: Applications need more than storage space. They need systems that structure data, retrieve it reliably, protect it, and handle multiple users safely.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: How does RAID support database availability?

choices.text: It prevents attackers from entering malicious SQL into input fields.
It uses multiple drives so one drive failure does not necessarily bring down the system.
It grants users permission to read, insert, update, or delete rows.
It encrypts every table with a cloud-managed key.
It separates tenants in a shared cloud database by unique tags.

questions.explanation: RAID improves availability at the physical storage layer. Mirroring can preserve another copy of data, while striping can improve performance, but RAID does not replace logical security controls.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What role does a DBMS play between an application and physical data?

choices.text: It replaces storage hardware by making disks unnecessary.
It gives every user full access to every table.
It acts as the software layer that creates, modifies, retrieves, and stores information safely.
It converts every cloud deployment into a private cloud.
It prevents all software bugs from reaching production.

questions.explanation: A DBMS abstracts direct disk access. Applications interact with a controlled software layer that knows how to find, update, and protect stored information.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: Why must a DBMS manage concurrent access?

choices.text: To ensure each hard drive receives a public IP address.
To allow two customers to buy the same limited item without conflict.
To make cloud APIs accessible without authentication.
To keep shared data accurate when multiple users or processes act at the same time.
To avoid creating primary keys or foreign keys.

questions.explanation: Concurrent access protects integrity. Locking records or ordering transactions prevents conflicting changes, such as processing two purchases for the same final seat.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: A database needs each customer row to be uniquely identifiable, orders to point to customers, and support staff to see only non-sensitive columns. Which relational tools match those needs?

choices.text: RAID striping for unique rows, cloud APIs for relationships, and ransomware backups for columns.
Grant commands for unique rows, revoke commands for relationships, and cookies for hidden columns.
Inband attacks for unique rows, blind attacks for relationships, and out-of-band attacks for columns.
Public clouds for unique rows, private clouds for relationships, and hybrid clouds for columns.
Primary keys for unique rows, foreign keys for relationships, and views for limited column access.

questions.explanation: Relational databases use different mechanisms for different structure needs. Primary keys identify rows, foreign keys connect tables, and views can expose only the columns a user should see.

questions.question: What problem does database access control solve?

choices.text: It controls who is allowed to see or change specific data.
It prevents every disk from failing physically.
It makes all cloud providers share the same infrastructure.
It eliminates the need for SQL commands.
It removes the need to structure data into tables.

questions.explanation: Access control protects confidentiality by ensuring users can perform only permitted actions on permitted data. It is a database-level control, not a storage redundancy mechanism.

questions.question: How does an authorization table enforce database permissions?

choices.text: It mirrors every disk so storage failures cannot occur.
It checks whether the user or role has permission for a requested action on specific data.
It rewrites unsafe SQL statements into safe placeholders.
It encrypts all database files without needing keys.
It moves each customer into a separate virtual machine.

questions.explanation: The authorization table works like a permissions list. When a user tries to read or modify data, the database checks whether that specific action is allowed.

questions.question: Which statement correctly compares centralized, ownership-based, and decentralized permission management?

choices.text: Centralized means every user controls all data; ownership-based means no one owns data; decentralized forbids department-level rules.
Centralized applies only to RAID; ownership-based applies only to cloud APIs; decentralized applies only to encryption keys.
Centralized uses one administrator or team; ownership-based lets data creators decide; decentralized lets teams manage access independently.
All three models assign permissions only through individual hard drives.
All three models prevent administrators from using grant or revoke commands.

questions.explanation: Permission administration can be organized in different ways. The right model affects consistency, local control, and how quickly access changes can be made.

questions.question: Which SQL privilege allows a user to modify existing data?

choices.text: Select.
Insert.
Delete.
Update.
References.

questions.explanation: Update is the privilege for changing existing data. Select reads data, Insert adds new data, Delete removes data, and References allows foreign key links.

questions.question: Why is RBAC safer and easier than assigning permissions separately to hundreds of support agents?

choices.text: RBAC lets every support agent grant themselves any permission they want.
RBAC removes the need to revoke access when employees leave.
RBAC works only when all users share the same password.
RBAC is a replacement for database tables and primary keys.
Permissions are attached to job roles, so access changes follow role changes instead of hundreds of individual edits.

questions.explanation: RBAC reduces administrative mistakes by managing permissions through roles such as Support Agent. When someone changes jobs or leaves, changing the role assignment updates access cleanly.

questions.question: What happens in a SQL injection attack?

choices.text: An attacker enters malicious SQL into a normal input field, and an unsafe application executes it.
A database mirrors data across several disks to survive drive failure.
A user receives a role that controls which tables they can read.
A cloud provider pools computing resources for many customers.
A DBMS locks records to preserve concurrent transaction integrity.

questions.explanation: SQLi abuses the boundary between input and code. If the application inserts unchecked input into a query, the database may execute attacker-supplied commands.

questions.question: Why can SQL injection threaten confidentiality, integrity, and availability at the same time?

choices.text: It only makes storage drives fail faster.
It can steal sensitive data, modify records, or delete the database.
It only slows down encrypted searches.
It only changes cloud billing rates.
It only affects users who already have administrator roles.

questions.explanation: SQLi is powerful because a database query can read, change, or destroy data. That means one flaw can expose secrets, corrupt information, and make the system unavailable.

questions.question: Which SQL injection technique requires the attacker to infer results from subtle clues such as page timing or behavior?

choices.text: Inband attack.
Out-of-band attack.
Inferential or blind attack.
Parameterized query.
Role-based access control.

questions.explanation: Blind attacks are used when the application does not directly show database output. The attacker must infer truth from differences in behavior or response time.

questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates second-order SQL injection?

choices.text: A developer uses placeholders so input is treated only as data.
A database uses RAID mirroring after a hard drive failure.
A user is assigned to a Support Agent role.
An attacker stores malicious SQL as a username, and it executes later when an administrator views the record.
A provider runs each customer's database on a separate virtual machine.

questions.explanation: Second-order injection is delayed. The dangerous value is accepted and stored first, then becomes active later when another process builds a query from that stored value.

questions.question: Why are parameterized queries the most effective SQL injection defense listed?

choices.text: They allow users to paste SQL directly into login fields safely.
They encrypt the whole database without any key management.
They move all databases into a private cloud.
They remove the need to scan code for weaknesses.
They force user input to be treated as data rather than executable SQL code.

questions.explanation: Parameterized queries separate query structure from input values. The database receives the user's input as a value, not as commands to execute, which blocks the core SQLi mechanism.

questions.question: What role does database encryption play if an attacker steals database files?

choices.text: It makes the stolen data unreadable without the correct mathematical key.
It prevents the attacker from ever copying the files.
It automatically restores missing records from RAID.
It grants users Select and Update privileges.
It converts public clouds into private clouds.

questions.explanation: Encryption is a last line of defense for confidentiality. It does not stop theft by itself, but it can make stolen data useless without the key.

questions.question: Which encryption scope is most consistent with selectively protecting highly sensitive data?

choices.text: Encrypting no data because access controls always prevent theft.
Encrypting only columns such as passwords and credit card numbers.
Encrypting only public information that anyone can read.
Encrypting only database diagrams and training manuals.
Encrypting only cloud provider marketing pages.

questions.explanation: Organizations often choose targeted encryption because encryption has costs. Sensitive columns such as passwords or payment data often deserve stronger protection than low-risk fields.

questions.question: Why is key management a critical weakness if handled poorly?

choices.text: Keys are needed only for RAID striping and never for decryption.
Keys become public automatically when a database is encrypted.
Losing the key can make the data unrecoverable, while stolen keys can make encryption useless.
Key management matters only for public clouds, not databases.
Stolen keys improve security by proving the attacker has the files.

questions.explanation: Encryption shifts part of the security problem to protecting keys. If keys are lost, the owner may lose access; if keys are stolen with the database, the attacker can decrypt the data.

questions.question: Why can encryption make database operations slower or less flexible?

choices.text: Encryption prevents all users from logging into the application.
Encryption requires every table to use no primary keys.
Encrypted data cannot be stored on physical drives.
Searching, filtering, sorting, and processing scrambled text is harder for applications.
Encryption forces the database into a multi-tenant architecture.

questions.explanation: Encrypted values are harder to work with because they are intentionally transformed. This security benefit can reduce query flexibility and performance.

questions.question: What does cloud computing allow an organization to do?

choices.text: Avoid all security responsibilities by outsourcing every decision.
Run databases only on physical servers owned by the organization.
Use software without any network access.
Store data without considering availability, integrity, or confidentiality.
Rent computing resources such as servers, storage, and databases over the internet.

questions.explanation: Cloud computing changes the ownership model. Instead of buying and maintaining hardware directly, organizations rent resources from providers and access them over the internet.

questions.question: Which cloud feature lets resources grow during a traffic spike and shrink afterward?

choices.text: Elasticity.
Resource pooling.
On-demand self-service.
Measured service.
Broad network access.

questions.explanation: Elasticity means capacity can expand and contract with demand. This is different from measured service, which is about paying for actual consumption.

questions.question: Which cloud service model gives the customer the most control but also requires the most effort?

choices.text: Platform as a Service.
Infrastructure as a Service.
Software as a Service.
Community Cloud.
Security as a Service.

questions.explanation: IaaS provides raw infrastructure, so the customer controls more but must install and manage more. SaaS is the opposite: convenient but with much less direct control.

questions.question: Which deployment model best fits a company that keeps highly sensitive data in dedicated infrastructure but runs a public website on shared provider resources?

choices.text: Public cloud.
Private cloud.
Hybrid cloud.
Community cloud.
Single-tenant SaaS.

questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private and public cloud use. It lets an organization place sensitive workloads in a more controlled environment while using public cloud for less sensitive or scalable services.

questions.question: A team wants to deploy code without managing servers or operating systems, but still needs to control its application and database design. Which service model best fits?

choices.text: IaaS, because it requires the customer to install the operating system.
SaaS, because the customer only logs into a finished application.
Public cloud, because deployment models define exactly what software layer is rented.
PaaS, because the provider handles servers and the OS while the team deploys its application.
Community cloud, because all organizations need identical application code.

questions.explanation: PaaS sits between IaaS and SaaS. It gives developers an environment for their code while shifting server and operating system management to the provider.

questions.question: Why does moving data to cloud infrastructure create an unknown risk profile?

choices.text: The customer owns all provider hardware after the first monthly payment.
Cloud providers cannot use access controls or monitoring.
Cloud resources are never managed through APIs.
Cloud systems cannot store sensitive data.
The customer cannot directly inspect the provider's physical servers and must trust the provider's security practices.

questions.explanation: Cloud use introduces trust in the provider. Customers often cannot verify physical server controls directly, so provider governance and transparency become important security factors.

questions.question: What is the main security concern with shared technology in public clouds?

choices.text: A failure in isolation software could let one customer's data become visible to another.
Shared hardware prevents cloud providers from pooling resources.
Shared technology guarantees stronger isolation than dedicated systems.
Shared technology removes all need for customer authentication.
Shared hardware prevents attackers from renting cloud resources.

questions.explanation: Public cloud efficiency comes from shared infrastructure. The security risk is that software isolation must work correctly to keep one tenant from seeing another tenant's data.

questions.question: Why are insecure interfaces, stolen API keys, and account hijacking especially dangerous in cloud environments?

choices.text: Cloud resources can be changed only by physically entering the provider's data center.
Cloud resources are controlled over the internet, so compromised credentials can expose or delete infrastructure remotely.
API keys can only read public documentation and cannot affect infrastructure.
Account hijacking affects only local text files, not cloud databases.
Cloud administrators do not use accounts or interfaces.

questions.explanation: Cloud management happens through online interfaces and APIs. If attackers gain those credentials, they may control large parts of the environment without physical access.

questions.question: Why can cloud convenience increase the chance of accidental data loss or leakage?

choices.text: Because cloud providers forbid all database configuration changes.
Because cloud databases cannot be reached over networks.
Because resources are easy to create and change, developers may accidentally expose databases to the public internet.
Because public cloud systems always encrypt every field with customer-held keys.
Because data leakage can happen only from physical disk failure.

questions.explanation: Speed and self-service are useful, but they create room for fast mistakes. A misconfigured cloud database can become publicly reachable before anyone notices.

questions.question: What does shared responsibility mean in cloud security?

choices.text: The provider is responsible for every customer security decision.
The customer owns the provider's physical hardware.
Security no longer matters because resources are rented.
Security duties are divided between the cloud provider and the customer.
Every tenant can administer every other tenant's data.

questions.explanation: Cloud customers do not own the physical hardware, but they still make important configuration, access, and data protection decisions. Security is therefore shared, not outsourced entirely.

questions.question: What is the main security advantage of a multi-instance cloud database model?

choices.text: All tenants share one database and depend entirely on tags.
The provider avoids using virtual machines.
Customers lose the ability to customize their environment.
The model always costs less because resources are shared.
Each customer receives an isolated database system on its own virtual machine.

questions.explanation: Multi-instance designs provide stronger isolation because each customer has a dedicated database environment. The trade-off is higher resource use and cost.

questions.question: Why is a multi-tenant database model efficient but potentially risky?

choices.text: Customers share one underlying database, so cost is lower but security depends on software correctly separating tenant data.
Each customer gets a dedicated database, so cost is low but customization is impossible.
Tenants share no infrastructure, so isolation logic does not matter.
It works only when every tenant has identical public data.
It eliminates the provider's responsibility for database security.

questions.explanation: Multi-tenant systems gain efficiency by sharing resources. The security risk is that tenant separation depends on correct software logic, so a flaw could expose another tenant's data.

questions.question: What is Security as a Service?

choices.text: A model where every customer must buy local hardware firewalls for each office.
A model where a provider delivers security applications over the internet using a SaaS approach.
A model where cloud providers stop scanning files or traffic.
A model where tenants manually inspect provider data centers.
A model where databases cannot use IAM or cloud firewalls.

questions.explanation: SECaaS applies the cloud service model to security tools. Instead of managing every tool locally, an organization can route traffic through or use services from a cloud security provider.

questions.question: Which set contains examples of SECaaS capabilities?

choices.text: Primary keys, foreign keys, views, and RAID striping.
Inband attacks, blind attacks, out-of-band attacks, and second-order injection.
IAM, cloud firewalls, threat detection, data loss prevention, and backup or disaster recovery.
Public cloud, private cloud, hybrid cloud, and community cloud.
Select, insert, delete, update, and references privileges only.

questions.explanation: SECaaS can include cloud-delivered identity management, filtering, scanning, DLP, and recovery services. These protect both cloud resources and local office networks.

questions.question: Why might an organization route traffic through a cloud security provider?

choices.text: To expose every internal database directly to the public internet.
To remove all need for identity and access management.
To avoid monitoring outgoing traffic for sensitive data.
To filter malicious traffic, scan activity, or apply security controls before traffic reaches applications.
To make local office networks impossible to protect.

questions.explanation: SECaaS can centralize and scale protective controls. Routing traffic through a provider can apply filtering, detection, and DLP before threats reach local or cloud systems.

questions.question: A healthcare customer requires strong isolation and deep database customization, while cost is less important. Which provider architecture is the better fit?

choices.text: Multi-tenant, because shared tags always provide the strongest possible isolation.
Public SaaS only, because finished applications always expose database configuration.
Second-order injection, because delayed execution improves isolation.
Measured service, because billing features determine database separation.
Multi-instance, because dedicated isolated systems support stronger separation and customization.

questions.explanation: Multi-instance fits when isolation and customization matter more than cost. Multi-tenant is efficient, but its security depends on shared software separation logic.

coverage.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
